sentences.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
sentences.text:
Ah, barbarous villains! Hath this lovely face
Ruled, like a wandering planet, over me,
And could it not enforce them to relent,
That were unworthy to behold the same?

sentences.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
sentences.text:
Ah, barbarous villains! Hath this lovely face
Ruled, like a wandering planet, over me,
And could it not enforce them to relent,
That were unworthy to behold the same?

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY VI
sentences.text: Lord Say, Jack Cade hath sworn to have thy head.

sentences.speaker: SAY
sentences.text: Ay, but I hope your highness shall have his.

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY VI
sentences.text:
How now, madam!
Still lamenting and mourning for Suffolk's death?
I fear me, love, if that I had been dead,
Thou wouldst not have mourn'd so much for me.

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY VI
sentences.text:
How now, madam!
Still lamenting and mourning for Suffolk's death?
I fear me, love, if that I had been dead,
Thou wouldst not have mourn'd so much for me.

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY VI
sentences.text:
How now, madam!
Still lamenting and mourning for Suffolk's death?
I fear me, love, if that I had been dead,
Thou wouldst not have mourn'd so much for me.

sentences.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
sentences.text: No, my love, I should not mourn, but die for thee.

sentences.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
sentences.text: No, my love, I should not mourn, but die for thee.
Enter a Messenger

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY VI
sentences.text: How now! What news? Why comest thou in such haste?

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY VI
sentences.text: How now! What news? Why comest thou in such haste?

sentences.speaker: MESSENGER
sentences.text:
The rebels are in Southwark; fly, my lord!
Jack Cade proclaims himself Lord Mortimer,
Descended from the Duke of Clarence' house,
And calls your grace usurper openly
And vows to crown himself in Westminster.
His army is a ragged multitude
Of hinds and peasants, rude and merciless:
Sir Humphrey Stafford and his brother's death
Hath given them heart and courage to proceed:
All scholars, lawyers, courtiers, gentlemen,
They call false caterpillars, and intend their death.

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY VI
sentences.text: O graceless men! They know not what they do.

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY VI
sentences.text: O graceless men! They know not what they do.

sentences.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
sentences.text:
My gracious lord, return to Killingworth,
Until a power be raised to put them down.

sentences.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
sentences.text:
Ah, were the Duke of Suffolk now alive,
These Kentish rebels would be soon appeased!

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY VI
sentences.text:
Lord Say, the traitors hate thee;
Therefore away with us to Killingworth.

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY VI
sentences.text:
Lord Say, the traitors hate thee;
Therefore away with us to Killingworth.

sentences.speaker: SAY
sentences.text:
So might your grace's person be in danger.
The sight of me is odious in their eyes;
And therefore in this city will I stay
And live alone as secret as I may.

sentences.speaker: SAY
sentences.text:

The sight of me is odious in their eyes;
And therefore in this city will I stay
And live alone as secret as I may.

Enter another Messenger

sentences.speaker: MESSENGER
sentences.text:
Jack Cade hath gotten London bridge:
The citizens fly and forsake their houses:
The rascal people, thirsting after prey,
Join with the traitor, and they jointly swear
To spoil the city and your royal court.

sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
sentences.text: Then linger not, my lord, away, take horse.

sentences.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
sentences.text: Then linger not, my lord, away, take horse.

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY VI
sentences.text: Come, Margaret; God, our hope, will succor us.

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY VI
sentences.text: Come, Margaret; God, our hope, will succor us.

sentences.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
sentences.text: My hope is gone, now Suffolk is deceased.

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY VI
sentences.text: Farewell, my lord: trust not the Kentish rebels.

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY VI
sentences.text: Farewell, my lord: trust not the Kentish rebels.

sentences.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
sentences.text: Trust nobody, for fear you be betray'd.

sentences.speaker: SAY
sentences.text:
The trust I have is in mine innocence,
And therefore am I bold and resolute.
Exeunt
Enter SCALES upon the Tower, walking. Then enter two or three Citizens below
Enter SCALES upon the Tower, walking. Then enter two or three Citizens below

sentences.speaker: SCALES
sentences.text: How now! Is Jack Cade slain?

sentences.speaker: SCALES
sentences.text: How now! Is Jack Cade slain?

sentences.speaker: FIRST CITIZEN
sentences.text:
No, my lord, nor likely to be slain; for they have
won the bridge, killing all those that withstand
them: the lord mayor craves aid of your honour from
the Tower, to defend the city from the rebels.

sentences.speaker: FIRST CITIZEN
sentences.text:

sentences.text: Exeunt
Enter CADE and the rest, and strikes his staff on London-stone
Enter CADE and the rest, and strikes his staff on London-stone

sentences.speaker: CADE
sentences.text:
Now is Mortimer lord of this city. And here, sitting
upon London-stone, I charge and command that, of the
city's cost, the pissing-conduit run nothing but
claret wine this first year of our reign . And now
henceforward it shall be treason for any that calls
me other than Lord Mortimer.

sentences.speaker: CADE
sentences.text:

me other than Lord Mortimer.

Enter a Soldier, running

sentences.speaker: SOLDIER
sentences.text: Jack Cade! Jack Cade!

sentences.speaker: SOLDIER
sentences.text: Jack Cade! Jack Cade!

sentences.speaker: CADE
sentences.text: Knock him down there.

sentences.speaker: CADE
sentences.text: Knock him down there.

sentences.speaker: SMITH
sentences.text:
If this fellow be wise, he'll never call ye Jack
Cade more: I think he hath a very fair warning.

sentences.speaker: SMITH
sentences.text:
If this fellow be wise, he'll never call ye Jack
Cade more: I think he hath a very fair warning.

sentences.speaker: DICK
sentences.text:
My lord, there's an army gathered together in
Smithfield.

sentences.speaker: CADE
sentences.text:
Come, then, let's go fight with them; but first, go
and set London bridge on fire; and, if you can, burn
down the Tower too. Come, let's away.

sentences.speaker: CADE
sentences.text:

and set London bridge on fire; and, if you can, burn

Alarums. MATTHEW GOFFE is slain, and all the rest. Then enter CADE, with his company.

sentences.speaker: CADE
sentences.text:
So, sirs: now go some and pull down the Savoy;
others to the Inns of Court; down with them all.

sentences.speaker: CADE
sentences.text:
So, sirs: now go some and pull down the Savoy;
others to the Inns of Court; down with them all.

sentences.speaker: DICK
sentences.text: I have a suit unto your lordship.

sentences.speaker: CADE
sentences.text: Be it a lordship, thou shalt have it for that word.

sentences.speaker: DICK
sentences.text:
Only that the laws of England may come out of your
mouth.

sentences.speaker: HOLLAND
sentences.text:
[Aside] Mass, 'twill be sore law, then; for he was
thrust in the mouth with a spear, and 'tis not whole
yet.

sentences.speaker: SMITH
sentences.text:
[Aside] Nay, John, it will be stinking law for his
breath stinks with eating toasted cheese.

sentences.speaker: CADE
sentences.text:
I have thought upon it, it shall be so. Away, burn
all the records of the realm: my mouth shall be
the parliament of England.

sentences.speaker: CADE
sentences.text:
I have thought upon it, it shall be so. Away, burn
all the records of the realm: my mouth shall be
the parliament of England.

sentences.speaker: CADE
sentences.text:
I have thought upon it, it shall be so. Away, burn
all the records of the realm: my mouth shall be
the parliament of England.

sentences.speaker: HOLLAND
sentences.text:
[Aside] Then we are like to have biting statutes,
unless his teeth be pulled out.

sentences.speaker: CADE
sentences.text: And henceforward all things shall be in common.
Enter a Messenger

sentences.speaker: MESSENGER
sentences.text:
My lord, a prize, a prize! Here's the Lord Say,
which sold the towns in France ; he that made us pay
one and twenty fifteens, and one shilling to the
pound, the last subsidy.

sentences.speaker: MESSENGER
sentences.text:
My lord, a prize, a prize! Here's the Lord Say,
which sold the towns in France ; he that made us pay
one and twenty fifteens, and one shilling to the
pound, the last subsidy.

sentences.speaker: MESSENGER
sentences.text:
My lord, a prize, a prize! Here's the Lord Say,
which sold the towns in France ; he that made us pay
one and twenty fifteens, and one shilling to the
pound, the last subsidy.
Enter BEVIS, with Lord SAY

sentences.speaker: CADE
sentences.text:
Well, he shall be beheaded for it ten times. Ah,
thou say, thou serge, nay, thou buckram lord! Now
art thou within point-blank of our jurisdiction
regal. What canst thou answer to my majesty for
giving up of Normandy unto Mounsieur Basimecu, the
dauphin of France? Be it known unto thee by these
presence, even the presence of Lord Mortimer, that I
am the besom that must sweep the court clean of such
filth as thou art. Thou hast most traitorously
corrupted the youth of the realm in erecting a
grammar school; and whereas, before, our forefathers
had no other books but the score and the tally, thou
hast caused printing to be used, and, contrary to
the king, his crown and dignity, thou hast built a
paper-mill. It will be proved to thy face that thou
hast men about thee that usually talk of a noun and
a verb, and such abominable words as no Christian
ear can endure to hear. Thou hast appointed
justices of peace, to call poor men before them
about matters they were not able to answer.
Moreover, thou hast put them in prison ; and because
they could not read, thou hast hanged them; when,
indeed, only for that cause they have been most
worthy to live. Thou dost ride in a foot-cloth, dost
thou not?

sentences.text:
Well, he shall be beheaded for it ten times. Ah,
thou say, thou serge, nay, thou buckram lord! Now
art thou within point-blank of our jurisdiction
regal. What canst thou answer to my majesty for
giving up of Normandy unto Mounsieur Basimecu, the
dauphin of France? Be it known unto thee by these
presence, even the presence of Lord Mortimer, that I
am the besom that must sweep the court clean of such
filth as thou art. Thou hast most traitorously
corrupted the youth of the realm in erecting a
grammar school; and whereas, before, our forefathers
had no other books but the score and the tally, thou
hast caused printing to be used, and, contrary to
the king, his crown and dignity, thou hast built a
paper-mill. It will be proved to thy face that thou
hast men about thee that usually talk of a noun and
a verb, and such abominable words as no Christian
ear can endure to hear. Thou hast appointed
justices of peace, to call poor men before them
about matters they were not able to answer.
Moreover, thou hast put them in prison ; and because
they could not read, thou hast hanged them; when,
indeed, only for that cause they have been most
worthy to live. Thou dost ride in a foot-cloth, dost
thou not?

sentences.speaker: SAY
sentences.text: What of that?

sentences.speaker: CADE
sentences.text:
Marry, thou oughtest not to let thy horse wear a
cloak, when honester men than thou go in their hose
and doublets.

sentences.speaker: DICK
sentences.text:
And work in their shirt too; as myself, for example,
that am a butcher.

sentences.speaker: SAY
sentences.text: You men of Kent,—

sentences.speaker: DICK
sentences.text: What say you of Kent?

sentences.speaker: SAY
sentences.text: Nothing but this; 'tis 'bona terra, mala gens.'

sentences.speaker: CADE
sentences.text: Away with him, away with him! He speaks Latin.

sentences.speaker: CADE
sentences.text: Away with him, away with him! He speaks Latin.

sentences.speaker: SAY
sentences.text:
Hear me but speak, and bear me where you will.
Kent, in the Commentaries Caesar writ,
Is term'd the civil'st place of this isle:
Sweet is the country, because full of riches;
The people liberal, valiant, active, wealthy;
Which makes me hope you are not void of pity.
I sold not Maine, I lost not Normandy,
Yet, to recover them, would lose my life.
Justice with favour have I always done;
Prayers and tears have moved me, gifts could never.
When have I aught exacted at your hands,
But to maintain the king, the realm and you?
Large gifts have I bestow'd on learned clerks,
Because my book preferr'd me to the king,
And seeing ignorance is the curse of God,
Knowledge the wing wherewith we fly to heaven,
Unless you be possess'd with devilish spirits,
You cannot but forbear to murder me:
This tongue hath parley'd unto foreign kings
For your behoof,—

sentences.speaker: CADE
sentences.text: Tut, when struck'st thou one blow in the field?

sentences.speaker: SAY
sentences.text:
Great men have reaching hands: oft have I struck
Those that I never saw and struck them dead.

sentences.speaker: SAY
sentences.text:
Great men have reaching hands: oft have I struck
Those that I never saw and struck them dead.

sentences.speaker: BEVIS
sentences.text: O monstrous coward! What, to come behind folks?

sentences.speaker: BEVIS
sentences.text: O monstrous coward! What, to come behind folks?

sentences.speaker: SAY
sentences.text: These cheeks are pale for watching for your good.

sentences.speaker: CADE
sentences.text:
Give him a box o' the ear and that will make 'em red
again.

sentences.speaker: SAY
sentences.text:
Long sitting to determine poor men's causes
Hath made me full of sickness and diseases.

sentences.speaker: CADE
sentences.text:
Ye shall have a hempen caudle, then, and the help of
hatchet.

sentences.speaker: DICK
sentences.text: Why dost thou quiver, man?

sentences.speaker: SAY
sentences.text: The palsy, and not fear, provokes me.

sentences.speaker: CADE
sentences.text:
Nay, he nods at us, as who should say, I'll be even
with you: I'll see if his head will stand steadier
on a pole, or no. Take him away, and behead him.

sentences.speaker: CADE
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: SAY
sentences.text:
Tell me wherein have I offended most?
Have I affected wealth or honour? Speak.
Are my chests fill'd up with extorted gold?
Is my apparel sumptuous to behold?
Whom have I injured, that ye seek my death?
These hands are free from guiltless bloodshedding,
This breast from harbouring foul deceitful thoughts.
O, let me live!

sentences.speaker: CADE
sentences.text:
[Aside] I feel remorse in myself with his words;
but I'll bridle it: he shall die, an it be but for
pleading so well for his life. [Aloud.] Away with him!
He
has a familiar under his tongue; he speaks not o'
God's name. Go, take him away, I say, and strike
off his head presently; and then break into his
son-in-law's house, Sir James Cromer, and strike off
his head, and bring them both upon two poles hither.

God's name. Go, take him away, I say, and strike
off his head presently; and then break into his
son-in-law's house, Sir James Cromer, and strike off
his head, and bring them both upon two poles hither.

sentences.speaker: CADE

[Aside] I feel remorse in myself with his words;
but I'll bridle it: he shall die, an it be but for
pleading so well for his life. [Aloud.] Away with him!

sentences.speaker: SAY
sentences.text:
Ah, countrymen! If when you make your prayers,
God should be so obdurate as yourselves,
How would it fare with your departed souls?
And therefore yet relent, and save my life.

sentences.speaker: CADE
sentences.text: Away with him! And do as I command ye.
Exeunt some with Lord SAY

sentences.speaker: CADE
sentences.text:
The proudest peer in the realm shall not wear a head
on his shoulders, unless he pay me tribute; there
shall not a maid be married, but she shall pay to me
her maidenhead ere they have it: men shall hold of
me in capite; and we charge and command that their
wives be as free as heart can wish or tongue can tell.

sentences.speaker: ALL
sentences.text: O, brave!
Re-enter one with the heads

sentences.speaker: CADE
sentences.text:
But is not this braver? Let them kiss one another,
for they loved well when they were alive. Now part
them again, lest they consult about the giving up of
some more towns in France. Soldiers, defer the
spoil of the city until night: for with these borne
before us, instead of maces, will we ride through
the streets, and at every corner have them kiss. Away!

sentences.speaker: CADE

sentences.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
sentences.text:
Ay, here they be that dare and will disturb thee:
Know, Cade, we come ambassadors from the king
Unto the commons whom thou hast misled;
And here pronounce free pardon to them all
That will forsake thee and go home in peace.

sentences.speaker: BUCKINGHAM

sentences.speaker: CLIFFORD
sentences.text:
What say ye, countrymen? Will ye relent,
And yield to mercy whilst 'tis offer'd you;
Or let a rebel lead you to your deaths?
Who loves the king and will embrace his pardon,
Fling up his cap, and say 'God save his majesty!'
Who hateth him and honours not his father,
Henry the Fifth, that made all France to quake,
Shake he his weapon at us and pass by.

sentences.speaker: ALL
sentences.text: God save the king! God save the king!

sentences.speaker: ALL
sentences.text: God save the king! God save the king!

sentences.speaker: CADE
sentences.text:
What, Buckingham and Clifford, are ye so brave? And
you, base peasants, do ye believe him? Will you
needs be hanged with your pardons about your necks?
Hath my sword therefore broke through London gates,
that you should leave me at the White Hart in
Southwark? I thought ye would never have given out
these arms till you had recovered your ancient
freedom: but you are all recreants and dastards,
and delight to live in slavery to the nobility. Let
them break your backs with burthens, take your
houses over your heads, ravish your wives and
daughters before your faces: for me, I will make
shift for one; and so, God's curse light upon you
all!

sentences.speaker: ALL
sentences.text: We'll follow Cade, we'll follow Cade!

sentences.speaker: CLIFFORD
sentences.text:
Is Cade the son of Henry the Fifth,
That thus you do exclaim you'll go with him?
Will he conduct you through the heart of France,
And make the meanest of you earls and dukes?
Alas, he hath no home, no place to fly to;
Nor knows he how to live but by the spoil,
Unless by robbing of your friends and us.
Were't not a shame, that whilst you live at jar,
The fearful French, whom you late vanquished,
Should make a start o'er seas and vanquish you?
Methinks already in this civil broil
I see them lording it in London streets,
Crying 'Villiago!' unto all they meet.
Better ten thousand base-born Cades miscarry
Than you should stoop unto a Frenchman's mercy.
To France, to France, and get what you have lost;
Spare England, for it is your native coast;
Henry hath money, you are strong and manly;
God on our side, doubt not of victory.

sentences.speaker: ALL

A Clifford! A Clifford! We'll follow the king and
Clifford.

sentences.speaker: ALL

A Clifford! A Clifford! We'll follow the king and

sentences.speaker: ALL

A Clifford! A Clifford! We'll follow the king and

sentences.speaker: CADE
sentences.text:
Was ever feather so lightly blown to and fro as this
multitude? The name of Henry the Fifth hales them
to an hundred mischiefs, and makes them leave me
desolate. I see them lay their heads together to
surprise me. My sword make way for me, for here is
no staying. In despite of the devils and hell, have
through the very middest of you? And heavens and
honour be witness, that no want of resolution in me.
But only my followers' base and ignominious
treasons, makes me betake me to my heels.

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY VI
sentences.text:
Was ever king that joy'd an earthly throne,
And could command no more content than I?
No sooner was I crept out of my cradle
But I was made a king, at nine months old.
Was never subject long'd to be a king
As I do long and wish to be a subject.

As I do long and wish to be a subject.

Enter BUCKINGHAM and CLIFFORD

sentences.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
sentences.text: Health and glad tidings to your majesty!

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY VI
sentences.text:
Why, Buckingham, is the traitor Cade surprised?
Or is he but retired to make him strong?

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY VI
sentences.text:
Why, Buckingham, is the traitor Cade surprised?
Or is he but retired to make him strong?
Enter below, multitudes, with halters about their necks

sentences.speaker: CLIFFORD
CLIFFORD
KING HENRY VI
sentences.text:
Then, heaven, set ope thy everlasting gates,
To entertain my vows of thanks and praise!
Soldiers, this day have you redeemed your lives,
And show'd how well you love your prince and country:
Continue still in this so good a mind,
And Henry, though he be infortunate,
Assure yourselves, will never be unkind:
And so, with thanks and pardon to you all,
I do dismiss you to your several countries.

sentences.speaker: ALL
sentences.text: God save the king! God save the king!

sentences.speaker: ALL
sentences.text: God save the king! God save the king!
Enter a Messenger

sentences.speaker: MESSENGER
sentences.text:
Please it your grace to be advertised
The Duke of York is newly come from Ireland,
And with a puissant and a mighty power
Of gallowglasses and stout kerns
Is marching hitherward in proud array,
And still proclaimeth, as he comes along,
His arms are only to remove from thee
The Duke of Somerset, whom he terms traitor.

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY VI
sentences.text:
Thus stands my state, 'twixt Cade and York distress'd.
Like to a ship that, having 'scaped a tempest,
Is straightway calm'd and boarded with a pirate:
But now is Cade driven back, his men dispersed;
And now is York in arms to second him.
I pray thee, Buckingham, go and meet him,
And ask him what's the reason of these arms.
Tell him I'll send Duke Edmund to the Tower;
And, Somerset, we'll commit thee thither,
Until his army be dismiss'd from him.

sentences.speaker: SOMERSET
sentences.text:
My lord,
I'll yield myself to prison willingly,
Or unto death, to do my country good.

sentences.speaker: SOMERSET
sentences.text:
My lord,
I'll yield myself to prison willingly,
Or unto death, to do my country good.

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY VI
sentences.text:
In any case, be not too rough in terms;
For he is fierce and cannot brook hard language.

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY VI
sentences.text:
In any case, be not too rough in terms;
For he is fierce and cannot brook hard language.

sentences.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
sentences.text:
I will, my lord; and doubt not so to deal
As all things shall redound unto your good.

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY VI
sentences.text:
Come, wife, let's in, and learn to govern better;
For yet may England curse my wretched reign.

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY VI
sentences.text:

For yet may England curse my wretched reign.
Flourish. Exeunt
Flourish. Exeunt
Enter CADE

sentences.speaker: CADE
sentences.text:
Fie on ambition! Fie on myself, that have a sword,
and yet am ready to famish! These five days have I
hid me in these woods and durst not peep out, for
all the country is laid for me; but now am I so
hungry that if I might have a lease of my life for a
thousand years I could stay no longer. Wherefore,
on a brick wall have I climbed into this garden, to
see if I can eat grass, or pick a sallet another
while, which is not amiss to cool a man's stomach
this hot weather. And I think this word 'sallet'
was born to do me good: for many a time, but for a
sallet, my brainpan had been cleft with a brown
bill; and many a time, when I have been dry and
bravely marching, it hath served me instead of a
quart pot to drink in; and now the word 'sallet'
must serve me to feed on.

Enter IDEN

sentences.speaker: IDEN
sentences.text:
Lord, who would live turmoiled in the court,
And may enjoy such quiet walks as these?
This small inheritance my father left me
Contenteth me, and worth a monarchy.
I seek not to wax great by others' waning,
Or gather wealth, I care not, with what envy:
Sufficeth that I have maintains my state
And sends the poor well pleased from my gate.

sentences.speaker: CADE
sentences.text:
Here's the lord of the soil come to seize me for a
stray, for entering his fee-simple without leave.
Ah, villain, thou wilt betray me, and get a thousand
crowns of the king carrying my head to him: but
I'll make thee eat iron like an ostrich, and swallow
my sword like a great pin, ere thou and I part.

sentences.speaker: IDEN
sentences.text:
Why, rude companion, whatsoe'er thou be,
I know thee not; why, then, should I betray thee?
Is't not enough to break into my garden,
And, like a thief, to come to rob my grounds,
Climbing my walls in spite of me the owner,
But thou wilt brave me with these saucy terms?

sentences.speaker: CADE
sentences.text:
Brave thee! Ay, by the best blood that ever was
broached, and beard thee too. Look on me well: I
have eat no meat these five days; yet, come thou and
thy five men, and if I do not leave you all as dead
as a doornail, I pray God I may never eat grass more.

sentences.speaker: IDEN
sentences.text:
Nay, it shall ne'er be said, while England stands,
That Alexander Iden, an esquire of Kent,
Took odds to combat a poor famish'd man.
Oppose thy steadfast-gazing eyes to mine,
See if thou canst outface me with thy looks:
Set limb to limb, and thou art far the lesser;
Thy hand is but a finger to my fist,
Thy leg a stick compared with this truncheon;
My foot shall fight with all the strength thou hast;
And if mine arm be heaved in the air,
Thy grave is digg'd already in the earth.
As for words, whose greatness answers words,
Let this my sword report what speech forbears.

sentences.speaker: CADE
sentences.text:
By my valour, the most complete champion that ever I
heard! Steel, if thou turn the edge, or cut not out
the burly-boned clown in chines of beef ere thou
sleep in thy sheath, I beseech God on my knees thou
mayst be turned to hobnails.

sentences.speaker: CADE
sentences.text:

Here they fight. CADE falls
Here they fight. CADE falls

sentences.speaker: CADE
sentences.text:
O, I am slain! Famine and no other hath slain me:
let ten thousand devils come against me, and give me
but the ten meals I have lost, and I'll defy them
all. Wither, garden; and be henceforth a
burying-place to all that do dwell in this house,
because the unconquered soul of Cade is fled.

sentences.text:
O, I am slain! Famine and no other hath slain me:
let ten thousand devils come against me, and give me
but the ten meals I have lost, and I'll defy them
all. Wither, garden; and be henceforth a
burying-place to all that do dwell in this house,
because the unconquered soul of Cade is fled.

sentences.speaker: IDEN
sentences.text:
Is't Cade that I have slain, that monstrous traitor?
Sword, I will hollow thee for this thy deed,
And hang thee o'er my tomb when I am dead:
Ne'er shall this blood be wiped from thy point;
But thou shalt wear it as a herald's coat,
To emblaze the honour that thy master got.

Dies

sentences.speaker: IDEN
sentences.text:
How much thou wrong'st me, heaven be my judge.
Die, damned wretch, the curse of her that bare thee;
And as I thrust thy body in with my sword,
So wish I, I might thrust thy soul to hell.
Hence will I drag thee headlong by the heels
Unto a dunghill which shall be thy grave,
And there cut off thy most ungracious head;
Which I will bear in triumph to the king,
Leaving thy trunk for crows to feed upon.

Enter YORK, and his army of Irish, with drum and colours

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text:
From Ireland thus comes York to claim his right,
And pluck the crown from feeble Henry's head:
Ring, bells, aloud; burn, bonfires, clear and bright,
To entertain great England's lawful king.
Ah! Sancta majestas, who would not buy thee dear?
Let them obey that know not how to rule;
This hand was made to handle naught but gold.
I cannot give due action to my words,
Except a sword or sceptre balance it:
A sceptre shall it have, have I a soul,
On which I'll toss the flower-de-luce of France.

Enter BUCKINGHAM

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text:
[Aside ] Whom have we here? Buckingham, to disturb me?
The king hath sent him, sure: I must dissemble.

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text:
[Aside ] Whom have we here? Buckingham, to disturb me?
The king hath sent him, sure: I must dissemble.

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text:
[Aside ] Whom have we here? Buckingham, to disturb me?
The king hath sent him, sure: I must dissemble.

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text:
[Aside ] Whom have we here? Buckingham, to disturb me?
The king hath sent him, sure: I must dissemble.

sentences.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
sentences.text: York, if thou meanest well, I greet thee well.

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text:
Humphrey of Buckingham, I accept thy greeting.
Art thou a messenger, or come of pleasure?

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text:
Humphrey of Buckingham, I accept thy greeting.
Art thou a messenger, or come of pleasure?

sentences.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
sentences.text:
A messenger from Henry, our dread liege,
To know the reason of these arms in peace;
Or why thou, being a subject as I am,
Against thy oath and true allegiance sworn,
Should raise so great a power without his leave,
Or dare to bring thy force so near the court.

sentences.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
sentences.text:
A messenger from Henry, our dread liege,
To know the reason of these arms in peace;
Or why thou, being a subject as I am,
Against thy oath and true allegiance sworn,
Should raise so great a power without his leave,
Or dare to bring thy force so near the court.

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text:
[Aside] Scarce can I speak, my choler is so great:
O, I could hew up rocks and fight with flint,
I am so angry at these abject terms;
And now, like Ajax Telamonius,
On sheep or oxen could I spend my fury.
I am far better born than is the king,
More like a king, more kingly in my thoughts:
But I must make fair weather yet a while,
Till Henry be more weak and I more strong,—
Buckingham, I prithee, pardon me,
That I have given no answer all this while;
My mind was troubled with deep melancholy.
The cause why I have brought this army hither
Is to remove proud Somerset from the king,
Seditious to his grace and to the state.

sentences.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
sentences.text:
That is too much presumption on thy part:
But if thy arms be to no other end,
The king hath yielded unto thy demand:
The Duke of Somerset is in the Tower.

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text: Upon thine honour, is he prisoner?

sentences.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
sentences.text: Upon mine honour, he is prisoner.

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text:
Then, Buckingham, I do dismiss my powers.
Soldiers, I thank you all; disperse yourselves;
Meet me to-morrow in St. George's field,
You shall have pay and every thing you wish.
And let my sovereign, virtuous Henry,
Command my eldest son, nay, all my sons,
As pledges of my fealty and love;
I'll send them all as willing as I live:
Lands, goods, horse, armour, any thing I have,
Is his to use, so Somerset may die.

sentences.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
sentences.text:
York, I commend this kind submission:
We twain will go into his highness' tent.

sentences.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
sentences.text:
York, I commend this kind submission:
We twain will go into his highness' tent.
Enter KING HENRY VI and Attendants

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY VI
sentences.text:
Buckingham, doth York intend no harm to us,
That thus he marcheth with thee arm in arm?

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text:
In all submission and humility
York doth present himself unto your highness.

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY VI
sentences.text: Then what intends these forces thou dost bring?

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text:
To heave the traitor Somerset from hence,
And fight against that monstrous rebel Cade,
Who since I heard to be discomfited.
Enter IDEN, with CADE'S head

sentences.speaker: IDEN
sentences.text:
If one so rude and of so mean condition
May pass into the presence of a king,
Lo, I present your grace a traitor's head,
The head of Cade, whom I in combat slew.

sentences.speaker: IDEN
sentences.text:
If one so rude and of so mean condition
May pass into the presence of a king,
Lo, I present your grace a traitor's head,
The head of Cade, whom I in combat slew.

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY VI
sentences.text:
The head of Cade! Great God, how just art Thou!
O, let me view his visage, being dead,
That living wrought me such exceeding trouble.
Tell me, my friend, art thou the man that slew him?

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY VI
sentences.text:

O, let me view his visage, being dead,

sentences.speaker: IDEN
sentences.text: I was, an't like your majesty.

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY VI
sentences.text: How art thou call'd? And what is thy degree?

sentences.speaker: IDEN
sentences.text:
Alexander Iden, that's my name;
A poor esquire of Kent, that loves his king.

sentences.speaker: IDEN
sentences.text:
Alexander Iden, that's my name;
A poor esquire of Kent, that loves his king.

sentences.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
sentences.text:
So please it you, my lord, 'twere not amiss
He were created knight for his good service.

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY VI
sentences.text: Iden, kneel down.
He kneels

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY VI
sentences.text:
Rise up a knight.
We give thee for reward a thousand marks,
And will that thou henceforth attend on us.

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY VI
sentences.text:
Rise up a knight.
We give thee for reward a thousand marks,
And will that thou henceforth attend on us.

sentences.speaker: IDEN
sentences.text:
May Iden live to merit such a bounty.
And never live but true unto his liege!

sentences.speaker: IDEN
sentences.text:
May Iden live to merit such a bounty.
And never live but true unto his liege!
Enter QUEEN MARGARET and SOMERSET

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY VI
sentences.text:
See, Buckingham, Somerset comes with the queen:
Go, bid her hide him quickly from the duke.

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY VI
sentences.text:
See, Buckingham, Somerset comes with the queen:
Go, bid her hide him quickly from the duke.

sentences.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
sentences.text:
For thousand Yorks he shall not hide his head,
But boldly stand and front him to his face.

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text:
How now! Is Somerset at liberty?
Then, York, unloose thy long-imprison'd thoughts,
And let thy tongue be equal with thy heart.
Shall I endure the sight of Somerset?
False king! Why hast thou broken faith with me,
Knowing how hardly I can brook abuse?
King did I call thee? No, thou art not king,
Not fit to govern and rule multitudes,
Which darest not, no, nor canst not rule a traitor.
That head of thine doth not become a crown;
Thy hand is made to grasp a palmer's staff,
And not to grace an awful princely sceptre.
That gold must round engirt these brows of mine,
Whose smile and frown, like to Achilles' spear,
Is able with the change to kill and cure.
Here is a hand to hold a sceptre up
And with the same to act controlling laws.
Give place: by heaven, thou shalt rule no more
O'er him whom heaven created for thy ruler.

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text:
How now! Is Somerset at liberty?
Then, York, unloose thy long-imprison'd thoughts,
And let thy tongue be equal with thy heart.
Shall I endure the sight of Somerset?
False king! Why hast thou broken faith with me,
Knowing how hardly I can brook abuse?
King did I call thee? No, thou art not king,
Not fit to govern and rule multitudes,
Which darest not, no, nor canst not rule a traitor.
That head of thine doth not become a crown;
Thy hand is made to grasp a palmer's staff,
And not to grace an awful princely sceptre.
That gold must round engirt these brows of mine,
Whose smile and frown, like to Achilles' spear,
Is able with the change to kill and cure.
Here is a hand to hold a sceptre up
And with the same to act controlling laws.
Give place: by heaven, thou shalt rule no more
O'er him whom heaven created for thy ruler.

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text:
I know, ere they will have me go to ward,
They'll pawn their swords for my enfranchisement.

sentences.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
sentences.text:
Call hither Clifford! Bid him come amain,
To say if that the bastard boys of York
Shall be the surety for their traitor father.

sentences.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
sentences.text:

Exit BUCKINGHAM

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text:
O blood-besotted Neapolitan,
Outcast of Naples, England's bloody scourge!
The sons of York, thy betters in their birth,
Shall be their father's bail; and bane to those
That for my surety will refuse the boys!

sentences.text:
Ay, Clifford; a bedlam and ambitious humour
Makes him oppose himself against his king.

sentences.speaker: CLIFFORD
sentences.text:
He is a traitor; let him to the Tower,
And chop away that factious pate of his.

sentences.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
sentences.text:
He is arrested, but will not obey;
His sons, he says, shall give their words for him.

sentences.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text: Will you not, sons?

sentences.speaker: EDWARD
sentences.text: Ay, noble father, if our words will serve.

sentences.speaker: EDWARD
sentences.text: Ay, noble father, if our words will serve.

sentences.speaker: RICHARD
sentences.text: And if words will not, then our weapons shall.

sentences.speaker: CLIFFORD
sentences.text: Why, what a brood of traitors have we here!

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text:
Look in a glass, and call thy image so:
I am thy king, and thou a false-heart traitor.
Call hither to the stake my two brave bears,
That with the very shaking of their chains
They may astonish these fell-lurking curs:
Bid Salisbury and Warwick come to me.

sentences.speaker: CLIFFORD
sentences.text:
Are these thy bears? We'll bait thy bears to death.
And manacle the bear-ward in their chains,
If thou darest bring them to the baiting place.

sentences.speaker: CLIFFORD
sentences.text:

If thou darest bring them to the baiting place.

sentences.speaker: CLIFFORD

If thou darest bring them to the baiting place.

sentences.speaker: RICHARD
sentences.text:
Oft have I seen a hot o'erweening cur
Run back and bite, because he was withheld;
Who, being suffer'd with the bear's fell paw,
Hath clapp'd his tail between his legs and cried:
And such a piece of service will you do,
If you oppose yourselves to match Lord Warwick.

sentences.speaker: CLIFFORD
sentences.text:
Hence, heap of wrath, foul indigested lump,
As crooked in thy manners as thy shape!

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text: Nay, we shall heat you thoroughly anon.

sentences.speaker: CLIFFORD
sentences.text: Take heed, lest by your heat you burn yourselves.

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY VI
sentences.text:
Why, Warwick, hath thy knee forgot to bow?
Old Salisbury, shame to thy silver hair,
Thou mad misleader of thy brain-sick son!
What, wilt thou on thy death-bed play the ruffian,
And seek for sorrow with thy spectacles?
O, where is faith? O, where is loyalty?
If it be banish'd from the frosty head,
Where shall it find a harbour in the earth?
Wilt thou go dig a grave to find out war,
And shame thine honourable age with blood?
Why art thou old, and want'st experience?
Or wherefore dost abuse it, if thou hast it?
For shame! In duty bend thy knee to me
That bows unto the grave with mickle age.

sentences.speaker: SALISBURY
sentences.text:
My lord, I have consider'd with myself
The title of this most renowned duke;
And in my conscience do repute his grace
The rightful heir to England's royal seat.

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY VI
sentences.text: Hast thou not sworn allegiance unto me?

sentences.speaker: SALISBURY
sentences.text: I have.

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY VI
sentences.text: Canst thou dispense with heaven for such an oath?

sentences.speaker: SALISBURY
sentences.text:
It is great sin to swear unto a sin,
But greater sin to keep a sinful oath.
Who can be bound by any solemn vow
To do a murderous deed, to rob a man,
To force a spotless virgin's chastity,
To reave the orphan of his patrimony,
To wring the widow from her custom'd right,
And have no other reason for this wrong
But that he was bound by a solemn oath?

sentences.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
sentences.text: A subtle traitor needs no sophister.

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY VI
sentences.text: Call Buckingham, and bid him arm himself.

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text:
Call Buckingham, and all the friends thou hast,
I am resolved for death or dignity.

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text:
Call Buckingham, and all the friends thou hast,
I am resolved for death or dignity.

sentences.speaker: CLIFFORD
sentences.text: The first I warrant thee, if dreams prove true.

sentences.speaker: WARWICK
sentences.text:
You were best to go to bed and dream again,
To keep thee from the tempest of the field.

sentences.speaker: CLIFFORD
sentences.text:
I am resolved to bear a greater storm
Than any thou canst conjure up to-day;
And that I'll write upon thy burgonet,
Might I but know thee by thy household badge.

sentences.speaker: CLIFFORD
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: WARWICK
sentences.text:
Now, by my father's badge, old Nevil's crest,
The rampant bear chain'd to the ragged staff,
This day I'll wear aloft my burgonet,
As on a mountain top the cedar shows
That keeps his leaves in spite of any storm,
Even to affright thee with the view thereof.

sentences.speaker: CLIFFORD
sentences.text:
And from thy burgonet I'll rend thy bear
And tread it under foot with all contempt,
Despite the bear-ward that protects the bear.

sentences.speaker: YOUNG CLIFFORD
sentences.text:
And so to arms, victorious father,
To quell the rebels and their complices.

sentences.speaker: YOUNG CLIFFORD
sentences.text:
And so to arms, victorious father,
To quell the rebels and their complices.

sentences.speaker: RICHARD
sentences.text:
Fie! Charity, for shame! Speak not in spite,
For you shall sup with Jesu Christ to-night.

sentences.speaker: RICHARD
sentences.text:
Fie! Charity, for shame! Speak not in spite,
For you shall sup with Jesu Christ to-night.

sentences.speaker: RICHARD
sentences.text:
Fie! Charity, for shame! Speak not in spite,
For you shall sup with Jesu Christ to-night.

sentences.speaker: YOUNG CLIFFORD
sentences.text: Foul stigmatic, that's more than thou canst tell.

sentences.speaker: RICHARD
sentences.text: If not in heaven, you'll surely sup in hell.
Exeunt severally
Alarums to the battle. Enter WARWICK
Alarums to the battle. Enter WARWICK

sentences.speaker: WARWICK
sentences.text:
Clifford of Cumberland, 'tis Warwick calls:
And if thou dost not hide thee from the bear,
Now, when the angry trumpet sounds alarum
And dead men's cries do fill the empty air,
Clifford, I say, come forth and fight with me:
Proud northern lord, Clifford of Cumberland,
Warwick is hoarse with calling thee to arms.

sentences.speaker: WARWICK
sentences.text: How now, my noble lord? What, all afoot?

sentences.speaker: WARWICK
sentences.text: How now, my noble lord? What, all afoot?

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text:
The deadly-handed Clifford slew my steed,
But match to match I have encounter'd him
And made a prey for carrion kites and crows
Even of the bonny beast he loved so well.

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text:

But match to match I have encounter'd him

Enter CLIFFORD

sentences.speaker: WARWICK
sentences.text: Of one or both of us the time is come.

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text:
Hold, Warwick, seek thee out some other chase,
For I myself must hunt this deer to death.

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text:
Hold, Warwick, seek thee out some other chase,
For I myself must hunt this deer to death.

sentences.speaker: WARWICK
sentences.text:
Then, nobly, York; 'tis for a crown thou fight'st.
As I intend, Clifford, to thrive to-day,
It grieves my soul to leave thee unassail'd.

sentences.speaker: WARWICK
sentences.text:

As I intend, Clifford, to thrive to-day,

sentences.speaker: CLIFFORD
sentences.text: What seest thou in me, York? Why dost thou pause?

sentences.speaker: CLIFFORD
sentences.text: What seest thou in me, York? Why dost thou pause?

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text:
With thy brave bearing should I be in love,
But that thou art so fast mine enemy.

sentences.speaker: CLIFFORD
sentences.text:
Nor should thy prowess want praise and esteem,
But that 'tis shown ignobly and in treason.

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text:
So let it help me now against thy sword
As I in justice and true right express it.

sentences.speaker: CLIFFORD
sentences.text: My soul and body on the action both!

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text: A dreadful lay! Address thee instantly.

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text: A dreadful lay! Address thee instantly.
They fight, and CLIFFORD falls
They fight, and CLIFFORD falls

sentences.speaker: CLIFFORD
sentences.text: La fin couronne les oeuvres.
Dies

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text:
Thus war hath given thee peace, for thou art still.
Peace with his soul, heaven, if it be thy will!

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text:
Thus war hath given thee peace, for thou art still.
Peace with his soul, heaven, if it be thy will!
Exit
Enter YOUNG CLIFFORD

sentences.speaker: YOUNG CLIFFORD
sentences.text:
Shame and confusion! All is on the rout;
Fear frames disorder, and disorder wounds
Where it should guard. O war, thou son of hell,
Whom angry heavens do make their minister
Throw in the frozen bosoms of our part
Hot coals of vengeance! Let no soldier fly.
He that is truly dedicate to war
Hath no self-love, nor he that loves himself
Hath not essentially but by circumstance
The name of valour.

sentences.speaker: YOUNG CLIFFORD
sentences.text:
O, let the vile world end,
And the premised flames of the last day
Knit earth and heaven together!
Now let the general trumpet blow his blast,
Particularities and petty sounds
To cease! Wast thou ordain'd, dear father,
To lose thy youth in peace, and to achieve
The silver livery of advised age,
And, in thy reverence and thy chair-days, thus
To die in ruffian battle? Even at this sight
My heart is turn'd to stone: and while 'tis mine,
It shall be stony. York not our old men spares;
No more will I their babes: tears virginal
Shall be to me even as the dew to fire,
And beauty that the tyrant oft reclaims
Shall to my flaming wrath be oil and flax.
Henceforth I will not have to do with pity:
Meet I an infant of the house of York,
Into as many gobbets will I cut it
As wild Medea young Absyrtus did:
In cruelty will I seek out my fame.
Come, thou new ruin of old Clifford's house:
As did AEneas old Anchises bear,
So bear I thee upon my manly shoulders;
But then AEneas bare a living load,
Nothing so heavy as these woes of mine.

sentences.speaker: RICHARD
sentences.text:
So, lie thou there;
For underneath an alehouse' paltry sign,
The Castle in Saint Alban's, Somerset
Hath made the wizard famous in his death.
Sword, hold thy temper; heart, be wrathful still:
Priests pray for enemies, but princes kill.

sentences.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
sentences.text: Away, my lord! You are slow; for shame, away!

sentences.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
sentences.text: Away, my lord! You are slow; for shame, away!

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY VI
sentences.text: Can we outrun the heavens? Good Margaret, stay.

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY VI
sentences.text: Can we outrun the heavens? Good Margaret, stay.

sentences.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
sentences.text:
What are you made of? You'll nor fight nor fly:
Now is it manhood, wisdom and defence,
To give the enemy way, and to secure us
By what we can, which can no more but fly.

Alarum afar off

sentences.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
sentences.text:
If you be ta'en, we then should see the bottom
Of all our fortunes: but if we haply scape,
As well we may, if not through your neglect,
We shall to London get, where you are loved
And where this breach now in our fortunes made
May readily be stopp'd.

If you be ta'en, we then should see the bottom
Of all our fortunes: but if we haply scape,
As well we may, if not through your neglect,
We shall to London get, where you are loved
And where this breach now in our fortunes made
May readily be stopp'd.
Re-enter YOUNG CLIFFORD

sentences.speaker: YOUNG CLIFFORD
sentences.text:
But that my heart's on future mischief set,
I would speak blasphemy ere bid you fly:
But fly you must; uncurable discomfit
Reigns in the hearts of all our present parts.
Away, for your relief! And we will live
To see their day and them our fortune give:
Away, my lord, away!

Alarum. Retreat. Enter YORK, RICHARD, WARWICK, and Soldiers, with drum and colours
Alarum. Retreat. Enter YORK, RICHARD, WARWICK, and Soldiers, with drum and colours

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text:
Of Salisbury, who can report of him,
That winter lion, who in rage forgets
Aged contusions and all brush of time,
And, like a gallant in the brow of youth,
Repairs him with occasion? This happy day
Is not itself, nor have we won one foot,
If Salisbury be lost.

sentences.speaker: RICHARD
sentences.text:
My noble father,
Three times to-day I holp him to his horse,
Three times bestrid him; thrice I led him off,
Persuaded him from any further act:
But still, where danger was, still there I met him;
And like rich hangings in a homely house,
So was his will in his old feeble body.
But, noble as he is, look where he comes.

sentences.speaker: SALISBURY
sentences.text:
Now, by my sword, well hast thou fought to-day;
By the mass, so did we all. I thank you, Richard:
God knows how long it is I have to live;
And it hath pleased him that three times to-day
You have defended me from imminent death.
Well, lords, we have not got that which we have:
'Tis not enough our foes are this time fled,
Being opposites of such repairing nature.

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text:
I know our safety is to follow them;
For, as I hear, the king is fled to London,
To call a present court of parliament.
Let us pursue him ere the writs go forth.
What says Lord Warwick? Shall we after them?

sentences.speaker: WARWICK
sentences.text:
After them! Nay, before them, if we can.
Now, by my faith, lords, 'twas a glorious day:
Saint Alban's battle won by famous York
Shall be eternized in all age to come.
Sound drums and trumpets, and to London all:
And more such days as these to us befall!
